Psalm 82 places you in a courtroom where God Himself presides over an assembly of judges—human rulers appointed to administer justice on earth. God's indictment is devastating: these judges have corrupted their calling. They render verdicts unjustly. They favor the wicked over the innocent. They ignore the weak, the fatherless, the afflicted, and the oppressed. This is not a minor administrative failure. When judges abandon justice, entire foundations shake. The vulnerable have nowhere to turn. Darkness spreads. God's anger at such betrayal is unmistakable, and His words demand that these appointed rulers do what they were meant to do: defend the defenseless and rescue the needy from wickedness.

But then comes a turning point. God reminds these corrupt judges of something crucial about their identity: "You are gods; you are all sons of the Most High." This is not flattery. It is a statement of their calling and responsibility. God Himself appointed them to positions of authority. He delegated power to them specifically so that justice would flow through their decisions to protect the vulnerable. They were meant to image God's own character—His justice, His defense of the powerless, His opposition to evil. Instead, they have squandered that divine trust.

What follows is their reckoning. "Like mortals you will die, and like rulers you will fall." Despite their divine appointment and earthly authority, they are not eternal. They are not beyond judgment. Death will come for them, and with it, accountability before the One who appointed them. This is a hard word for anyone in power: your authority is delegated, temporary, and will be audited by God Himself. The psalm ends not with the judges' response but with a cry to the only Judge who will never fail: "Arise, O God, judge the earth, for all the nations are Your inheritance." The psalmist's confidence is not in human justice systems but in God's ultimate and final justice over all the earth.

This psalm confronts two realities you cannot escape. First, if you hold any position of influence or authority—as a parent, employer, leader, or citizen—you are accountable to God for how you treat the vulnerable. Second, you are mortal. Your power is borrowed, temporary, and will be called to account. The only Judge who matters is the One whose inheritance includes every nation and every heart.
